Just now

Press enter or click to view image in full size

Modern Angular applications often contain valuable business logic, premium features, internal tools, or sensitive resources. A common question developers face is:
How do you prevent unauthorized users from downloading an Angular module from the browser?
Many developers try to hide routes, disable buttons, or add client-side permission checks. However, these approaches only improve the user experience — they do not provide real security.
The browser is a public environment. Anything delivered to the browser can potentially be inspected, modified, or downloaded.
The correct approach is:
Authentication and authorization must happen on the server before any protected module or resource is delivered.
Angular should handle user experience and navigation control, but the backend must make the final security decision.
Why Angular Route Guards Are Not Enough
Angular provides powerful tools like:

• CanActivate

• CanMatch
